_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against AIDS Malignancy Consortium consensus guidelines, Cesarman E Nat Rev Dis Primers 2019, Bower M BHIVA guidance, and NCI PDQ Kaposi Sarcoma.

h1Title: Kaposi's Sarcoma: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Kaposi's sarcoma (KS) is a vascular tumor caused by human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8, also known as Kaposi sarcoma-associated herpesvirus, KSHV), most often appearing as red-purple or brown skin patches and nodules but able to involve lymph nodes, gut, and lungs. It occurs in four epidemiologic forms — classic (older Mediterranean and Eastern European men), endemic (sub-Saharan Africa), iatrogenic (organ-transplant recipients), and AIDS-related (HIV-positive patients). AIDS-related KS was the AIDS-defining illness most associated with the early epidemic and remains the most common HIV-associated cancer globally, accounting for an estimated 34,000 new cases per year. Antiretroviral therapy for HIV-positive KS dramatically improves outcomes — over 80% of patients with limited skin-only disease achieve sustained remission on ART alone.

keyStats.prevalence: ~34,000 new AIDS-related KS cases globally per year (GLOBOCAN 2022)
keyStats.demographics: AIDS-related and classic KS predominantly affect men (5-15x more than women); endemic KS more sex-balanced
keyStats.avgAge: Classic KS: peaks at 60-80 years; AIDS-related KS: peaks at 30-50 years
keyStats.globalCases: Highest incidence in sub-Saharan Africa, where endemic and AIDS-related forms together drive 80% of global burden

definition: Kaposi's sarcoma (ICD-10: C46) is a low-grade vascular malignancy of endothelial origin caused by infection with human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8 / KSHV), a gamma-2 herpesvirus discovered in 1994. The tumor arises from lymphatic endothelial cells that have been latently infected with HHV-8 and shows characteristic spindle-cell proliferation with slit-like vascular channels, hemorrhage, and hemosiderin deposition on histology. It is classified into four clinical-epidemiologic types: classic KS (indolent, lower-extremity disease in elderly Mediterranean, Eastern European, and Middle Eastern men); endemic (African) KS, a more aggressive form predating the HIV epidemic that affects all ages including children with lymphadenopathic disease; iatrogenic KS in organ transplant recipients on calcineurin inhibitors; and AIDS-related (epidemic) KS in HIV-positive individuals, which has been the defining cancer of the HIV pandemic. AIDS-related KS is staged by the AIDS Clinical Trials Group TIS system: Tumor extent (T0 limited / T1 extensive), Immune status (I0 CD4 >200 / I1 CD4 <200), and Systemic illness (S0 no systemic features / S1 systemic features present). Care is led by oncologists and infectious disease specialists, often in combined HIV-oncology clinics, with dermatology and palliative care contributing throughout.

typesClassification.description: Indolent form in elderly men of Mediterranean, Eastern European, or Middle Eastern descent. Slow-growing red-purple patches and plaques on lower legs and feet. Disease progression over decades; rarely fatal. Discovered and described by Moritz Kaposi in 1872.

Pre-HIV form endemic in equatorial Africa. Four subtypes: nodular (similar to classic), florid (locally aggressive), infiltrative (deep tissue and bone), and lymphadenopathic (mostly children, often without skin lesions, aggressive course).

Develops in transplant recipients on immunosuppression, particularly calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine, tacrolimus). Incidence ~200-fold higher in transplant patients than general population. Often regresses with switch to sirolimus or reduced immunosuppression.

Most common HIV-associated cancer globally. Strongly correlated with CD4 count and HIV viremia. ART has reduced US/European incidence by over 80%; remains high in sub-Saharan Africa where ART access is incomplete and HHV-8 seroprevalence exceeds 50%.

primarySymptoms: Painless red, purple, or brown skin patches or plaques on the lower limbs, trunk, face, or oral mucosa, typically flat early then becoming raised over weeks to months.
Multiple lesions appearing in symmetric distribution along skin tension lines (Blaschko or Langer lines), often the earliest distinguishing sign from solitary vascular tumors.
Oral lesions on the hard palate, gums, or tongue — present in up to 25% of AIDS-related KS at diagnosis and often the first sign of advanced disease.
Painful lymphedema, especially of the legs and genitalia, from lymphatic obstruction in advanced cutaneous or nodal KS.
Hemoptysis, persistent cough, or shortness of breath from pulmonary KS — a poor prognostic feature.
Gastrointestinal bleeding, abdominal pain, or weight loss from gut involvement (most often duodenum, stomach, or rectum) — frequently asymptomatic and found at endoscopy.
Generalized lymph node enlargement, more typical of African endemic and pediatric forms.
Mucocutaneous bleeding from oral or genital KS lesions, especially in advanced disease.
Constitutional B symptoms (fever, weight loss, night sweats) in extensive systemic disease — overlapping with HIV symptoms and multicentric Castleman disease.
earlyWarningSigns: A new painless purple or brown patch on the leg, foot, face, or hard palate of a person with HIV infection
Multiple small skin lesions appearing along the same line of the body — a clue to HHV-8-driven angiogenesis
CD4 count below 200/µL or detectable HIV viral load in a person already known to be HIV-positive
New skin lesion in an organ transplant recipient on cyclosporine or tacrolimus
Persistent lymphadenopathy in a child or young adult from sub-Saharan Africa or Mediterranean region
emergencySigns: Sudden shortness of breath or hemoptysis in a patient with KS — pulmonary KS or KS-associated multicentric Castleman disease requires urgent oncology evaluation
Massive gastrointestinal bleeding from gut KS lesions — needs urgent endoscopy and possible transfusion
Severe limb lymphedema with ulceration and superinfection — risk of cellulitis and sepsis
Sudden facial swelling and respiratory compromise from oropharyngeal KS — possible airway obstruction needing ENT involvement
High fever, cytopenias, and hepatosplenomegaly in a patient with KS — possible KS-associated multicentric Castleman disease or HHV-8 inflammatory cytokine syndrome (KICS)

causes.description: The necessary infectious cause, discovered by Chang and Moore in 1994. HHV-8 establishes lifelong latent infection in B-cells and endothelial cells. Seroprevalence varies sharply by geography: under 5% in most US/European populations, 20-50% in Mediterranean and Eastern European countries, and over 50% in much of sub-Saharan Africa.

HIV-induced CD4 depletion permits HHV-8 reactivation and drives endothelial cell transformation. KS risk in HIV-positive patients is 30,000-fold higher than baseline in some pre-ART cohorts; modern ART reduces this risk by over 90% but does not eliminate it.

Calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine, tacrolimus) confer KS risk 200-fold higher than the general population in transplant recipients. Mechanism involves both general T-cell suppression and a calcineurin-specific pro-angiogenic effect. Switching to sirolimus (mTOR inhibitor) often produces tumor regression.

HLA-DRB1*04 and DRB1*11 variants confer higher KS risk after HHV-8 infection. Other susceptibility loci including IL-6 promoter variants and immunogenetic factors partly explain the high incidence in Mediterranean populations and persistent endemic foci in Africa.

Classic KS clusters in Mediterranean and Eastern European men of Jewish, Italian, or Greek descent. Endemic KS persists in equatorial Africa. Same-sex sexual contact among men confers higher HHV-8 transmission rates. These distributions reflect HHV-8 transmission patterns rather than independent risk factors.

riskFactors.factor: HIV infection with low CD4 count
riskFactors.description: AIDS-related KS risk rises steeply below CD4 200/µL. Modern ART reduces incidence by over 90% in the era of universal viral suppression.
All KS cases are HHV-8-positive. Seroprevalence is region-dependent: under 5% in much of the US/Europe, 20-50% in Mediterranean countries, and over 50% in sub-Saharan Africa.

riskFactors.factor: Solid organ transplantation on calcineurin inhibitors
riskFactors.description: Risk approximately 200-fold higher than general population; KS develops in 0.5-5% of transplant recipients in endemic-HHV-8 regions. Switching to sirolimus often produces regression.

riskFactors.factor: Male sex and same-sex male contact
riskFactors.description: All forms of KS show male predominance (5-15x in classic and AIDS-related forms). HHV-8 transmission rates are highest among men who have sex with men, partly explaining the male skew of AIDS-related KS.

riskFactors.factor: Mediterranean, Eastern European, or sub-Saharan African ancestry
riskFactors.description: Geographic clustering reflects HHV-8 seroprevalence and immunogenetic susceptibility. Highest classic KS incidence is documented in Italian, Greek, Sephardic Jewish, and Middle Eastern men over 60.
Classic KS incidence rises with age and peaks at 60-80 years, presumably reflecting age-related immune senescence in HHV-8-positive individuals.

affectedDemographics: Sub-Saharan Africa carries roughly 80% of global KS burden; Eswatini, Zimbabwe, and Mozambique have the highest age-standardized incidence
US: ~6,000 cases per year; over 80% in HIV-positive individuals, with persistent disparity in Black and Hispanic populations
Pre-ART era HIV cohorts showed lifetime KS incidence of 15-30% in HIV-positive MSM; modern ART has reduced this to under 5%
Transplant recipients: KS incidence 200x higher than general population in HHV-8-endemic regions; <0.5% in low-prevalence regions
Endemic African KS affects all ages including children; pediatric lymphadenopathic form has poor prognosis without prompt chemotherapy
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines clinical recognition with histopathology. Suspicious skin or mucosal lesions undergo punch biopsy showing the hallmark histologic features: spindle-cell proliferation, slit-like vascular channels lined by atypical endothelial cells, extravasated red blood cells, hemosiderin deposition, and a lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate. Immunohistochemistry for HHV-8 latency-associated nuclear antigen (LANA-1) is essentially 100% sensitive and specific and is now considered required to confirm diagnosis. Once diagnosis is established, staging determines treatment. AIDS-related KS uses the AIDS Clinical Trials Group TIS classification: Tumor extent (T0 limited skin or oral / T1 extensive cutaneous, visceral, or tumor-associated edema), Immune status (I0 CD4 ≥200 / I1 CD4 <200), and Systemic illness (S0 / S1 with B symptoms, opportunistic infections, or KS-related visceral disease). Routine workup includes complete physical examination with documentation of skin lesion count and distribution, HIV testing and HIV viral load, CD4 count, complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel, HHV-8 PCR (in selected cases), chest imaging (CT in suspected pulmonary KS), and esophagogastroduodenoscopy plus colonoscopy in symptomatic patients or those with extensive disease. Differential diagnosis includes bacillary angiomatosis (Bartonella infection, treatable with antibiotics), pyogenic granuloma, melanoma, hemosiderotic dermatofibroma, and other vascular tumors. The combination of multiple symmetric purple-brown lesions in an HIV-positive person plus HHV-8 LANA-1 positivity on biopsy resolves nearly all cases.

diagnosticTests.test: Punch biopsy of suspicious skin or mucosal lesion
diagnosticTests.purpose: Reference standard for diagnosis. Histology shows spindle-cell proliferation, slit-like vascular channels, extravasated red blood cells, and hemosiderin. Immunohistochemistry for HHV-8 LANA-1 confirms the diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Local anesthetic, then 4-5 mm punch biopsy of an active lesion. Mild discomfort; one or two sutures. Results in 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms HHV-8 infection in tumor cells with near-100% sensitivity and specificity. Essential for distinguishing KS from histologic mimics including bacillary angiomatosis, pyogenic granuloma, and other vascular tumors.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on the biopsy specimen; no additional patient procedures required. Results returned with the histology report.

diagnosticTests.test: HIV testing and CD4 count plus HIV viral load
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies AIDS-related KS and stratifies immune status. CD4 count below 200/µL defines TIS Class I1 and influences chemotherapy choice and prognosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. HIV antibody/antigen and HIV RNA viral load return in 1-3 days; CD4 count typically same-day.

diagnosticTests.test: Chest CT or PET-CT for staging
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects pulmonary KS (peribronchovascular nodules, pleural effusions), mediastinal lymphadenopathy, and other visceral involvement. PET-CT increasingly used for treatment response assessment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard CT scan with or without contrast, taking 10-20 minutes. PET-CT requires fasting and a small radiotracer injection 60 minutes before imaging.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects gastrointestinal KS, which is often asymptomatic but affects up to 40% of patients with extensive cutaneous disease. Important for staging and identifying bleeding risk.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient procedure under sedation. EGD takes 15-30 minutes, colonoscopy 30-45 minutes. Biopsies can be taken at the time.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualization of typical KS lesions in the airways. Pulmonary KS has a characteristic endobronchial appearance; bronchial biopsy is often deferred due to bleeding risk from the highly vascular lesions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient procedure with local anesthesia and conscious sedation. The bronchoscope is passed through the nose or mouth; takes 30-45 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Treatment depends on KS subtype, extent, organ involvement, and immune status. For AIDS-related KS, the foundation is potent antiretroviral therapy (ART) regardless of CD4 count. ART alone produces sustained complete or partial response in 60-80% of patients with limited skin-only disease (TIS T0), and substantial regression in many T1 cases. ART should be started immediately or within 2 weeks of KS diagnosis in nearly all patients. For extensive disease (T1) or visceral involvement, chemotherapy is added concurrently with ART. Liposomal doxorubicin (Doxil) at 20 mg/m² IV every 2-3 weeks is the global first-line systemic agent, with response rates of 50-70%. Liposomal daunorubicin and paclitaxel are alternatives with similar efficacy. Modern phase-II studies of pomalidomide (oral immunomodulator) have shown response rates of 60-70% across HIV-positive and HIV-negative patients and have led to FDA accelerated approval as a second-line option. For classic KS, treatment is more conservative — many cases require only watchful waiting. Local therapies (intralesional vinblastine, topical alitretinoin, radiation, cryotherapy) are first-line for localized disease. Systemic chemotherapy is reserved for progressive or symptomatic disease. For iatrogenic KS, the cornerstone is switching from calcineurin inhibitors to mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus or everolimus); this produces complete remission in 50-70% of cases without additional chemotherapy. For endemic African KS, treatment access remains a major challenge; liposomal doxorubicin is gradually being introduced through AIDS Malignancy Consortium and partner programs, though oral chemotherapy (etoposide, vincristine) and radiation remain widely used due to cost and infusion logistics. Local radiation therapy (8-30 Gy in 1-10 fractions) is highly effective for painful or cosmetically prominent skin lesions, with complete response rates of 70-90%. Supportive care for lymphedema (compression garments, lymphatic drainage), pain management, and treatment of opportunistic infections are essential throughout. Immune checkpoint inhibitors and HHV-8-directed antivirals are under active investigation but not yet standard of care.

medicalTreatments.name: Antiretroviral therapy (ART) for HIV-positive KS — integrase inhibitor-based regimens (bictegravir/TAF/FTC or dolutegravir/TAF/FTC)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for all AIDS-related KS regardless of CD4 count. Start within 2 weeks of diagnosis. Modern single-tablet regimens have low side-effect profiles and consistently achieve viral suppression. Mechanism in KS includes both HHV-8 immune reconstitution and anti-angiogenic effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Sustained complete or partial KS response in 60-80% of T0 disease on ART alone (Bower 2014); substantial regression in T1 disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Liposomal doxorubicin (Doxil 20 mg/m² IV every 2-3 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Global first-line systemic chemotherapy for extensive or visceral KS. Pegylated liposomal formulation concentrates in tumor tissue and reduces cardiotoxicity vs conventional doxorubicin. Treatment continued until best response or 6-8 cycles.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Overall response rate 50-70% in HIV-associated KS; superior to conventional ABV (doxorubicin/bleomycin/vincristine) in randomized trials with lower toxicity.

medicalTreatments.name: Paclitaxel (100 mg/m² IV every 2 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Second-line for KS unresponsive to liposomal doxorubicin or as alternative first-line. Microtubule stabilizer with anti-angiogenic effects in KS. Requires premedication for hypersensitivity; neuropathy is a dose-limiting side effect.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Overall response rate 50-60% in pretreated KS (Tulpule 2002); non-inferior to liposomal doxorubicin in head-to-head trial.

medicalTreatments.name: Pomalidomide (5 mg orally daily on days 1-21 of 28-day cycle)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral immunomodulator with anti-angiogenic and anti-inflammatory effects. FDA-accelerated approval 2020 for KS after standard therapy failure. Useful in both HIV-positive and HIV-negative KS. Thalidomide-derivative requires pregnancy-prevention REMS program.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Overall response rate 60-70% across HIV-positive and HIV-negative KS in Polizzotto 2016 phase II trial; duration of response often exceeds 12 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Local radiation therapy (8-30 Gy in 1-10 fractions)
medicalTreatments.description: Highly effective for painful, ulcerated, or cosmetically prominent skin lesions. Single-fraction 8 Gy gives durable palliation; conventional fractionation (20-30 Gy in 5-10 fractions) achieves the highest complete response rates. Sparing for visceral disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete response rate 70-90% at irradiated sites; local control durable in most cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Intralesional vinblastine (0.1-0.2 mg/lesion)
medicalTreatments.description: Office-based treatment for limited classic or AIDS-related KS skin lesions. Vinblastine is injected directly into each lesion every 2-4 weeks until regression. Local skin irritation and hyperpigmentation are common; systemic absorption minimal.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete response in 60-80% of treated lesions; multiple sessions usually required.

medicalTreatments.name: Sirolimus (everolimus) for iatrogenic KS — switch from calcineurin inhibitor
medicalTreatments.description: mTOR inhibitor replacing cyclosporine or tacrolimus in transplant recipients with KS. Anti-angiogenic and growth-inhibitory effects on KS spindle cells. Doses titrated to therapeutic trough levels (typically 4-12 ng/mL).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete KS regression in 50-70% of iatrogenic KS within 3-6 months of conversion (Stallone 2005); allows continued transplant immunosuppression.

surgicalOptions.name: Excision of isolated symptomatic skin lesions
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for very limited classic KS where 1-2 painful or cosmetically prominent lesions can be removed cleanly. Not curative because of multifocal nature; recurrence at margins or distant sites is common.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Local clearance of excised lesion in 90%+; new lesions develop elsewhere in 60-80% over 5 years.

whySeeSpecialist: All suspected KS should be evaluated by an oncologist with HIV/oncology experience or in dedicated HIV-oncology multidisciplinary clinics. Infectious disease involvement is essential for AIDS-related KS to optimize ART and manage opportunistic infections. Transplant nephrologists/hepatologists guide iatrogenic KS management with sirolimus conversion. Dermatology contributes to skin lesion assessment and local therapy planning. Palliative care has a role throughout extensive disease.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an oncology practice with experience in HIV-associated malignancies; in the US these are commonly within AIDS Malignancy Consortium member institutions or academic HIV-oncology programs. Confirm access to liposomal doxorubicin and second-line agents including pomalidomide. For transplant patients, the transplant team should co-manage with oncology. Continuity matters — KS is a chronic relapsing condition in many patients.
hospitalCriteria: Established HIV-oncology multidisciplinary clinic with infectious disease and oncology integration
Access to liposomal doxorubicin, paclitaxel, and pomalidomide formularies
Radiation oncology service for palliative radiation of localized lesions
Pathology service capable of HHV-8 LANA-1 immunohistochemistry
Linkage to clinical trials through AIDS Malignancy Consortium or equivalent networks
keyFacilities: AIDS Malignancy Consortium centers (US National Cancer Institute network)
National Cancer Institute Division of Cancer Treatment HIV-Oncology Program
Chelsea and Westminster Hospital (UK) — long-established HIV oncology service
Uganda Cancer Institute — major sub-Saharan African KS research and treatment center
preventionStrategies: Sustained ART for all HIV-positive individuals, achieving and maintaining viral suppression — the single most effective intervention to prevent AIDS-related KS
Pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) and condom use to reduce HIV transmission in MSM and other high-risk groups
Use of sirolimus rather than calcineurin inhibitors in transplant recipients from HHV-8 endemic regions where feasible
HHV-8 transmission risk reduction — avoid shared saliva exposure with known HHV-8-positive partners (limited evidence)
Annual skin and oral examination for all HIV-positive patients and HHV-8-seropositive transplant recipients
lifestyleModifications: Adhere strictly to ART or transplant immunosuppression regimens as prescribed
Use sun protection — UV light may stimulate KS lesions
Manage lymphedema with compression garments, manual lymphatic drainage, and elevation
Treat skin infections promptly to prevent superinfection of fragile KS lesions
Maintain good oral hygiene; oral KS may bleed and is at risk of infection
Stop smoking — independent risk factor for lung cancer and reduces tolerance of chemotherapy

recommended: Balanced diet with adequate protein and calories to maintain weight during chemotherapy
Antioxidant-rich vegetables and fruits as part of a Mediterranean-style pattern
Adequate iron and folate to prevent worsening anemia during chemotherapy
Hydration adequate to support kidney function on nephrotoxic regimens
avoid: Excessive alcohol during chemotherapy due to additive hepatotoxicity
Raw or undercooked foods during chemotherapy-induced neutropenia
Grapefruit juice during sirolimus or calcineurin inhibitor therapy due to CYP3A4 interactions
Crash diets or extreme weight-loss attempts during active treatment

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise (walking, swimming, cycling) is encouraged throughout treatment. Compression garments and limb elevation help patients with lymphedema participate safely. Avoid contact sports during chemotherapy-induced thrombocytopenia or in patients with advanced cutaneous disease prone to bleeding. Strength training preserves muscle mass during treatment.
dailyManagement: Take ART or transplant medications at the same time every day without interruption
Inspect skin, oral cavity, and feet weekly for new lesions or changes; report changes promptly
Use prescribed compression garments daily if lymphedema is present
Attend all chemotherapy and oncology appointments; missed doses reduce efficacy
Keep an updated list of all medications and recent lab results when traveling
prognosis: Prognosis varies dramatically by subtype. Classic KS is indolent — 10-year survival exceeds 85% and most patients die with rather than from KS. Iatrogenic KS often regresses with conversion to sirolimus; mortality from KS is uncommon when conversion is feasible. AIDS-related KS in the modern ART era has 5-year overall survival of 75-85% in well-resourced settings, a dramatic improvement from the pre-ART era when median survival was under 18 months. Adverse prognostic features include CD4 count below 200/µL, pulmonary involvement, extensive lymphedema, and systemic B symptoms. Endemic African KS has the worst prognosis globally, primarily due to limited access to liposomal doxorubicin and pomalidomide; the lymphadenopathic pediatric form is particularly aggressive. Treatment response is durable in most: at 5 years post-treatment of HIV-associated KS, the relapse rate is 10-20% in patients with sustained viral suppression. The introduction of pomalidomide and the active investigation of immune checkpoint inhibitors are expected to further improve outcomes. Quality-of-life impacts include cosmetic concerns, lymphedema, oral lesions interfering with eating, and the chronic relapsing course in extensive disease.
recoveryTimeline: Cutaneous lesions begin to flatten and lose pigment within 4-8 weeks of effective ART or chemotherapy. Complete clinical response usually requires 4-6 months of treatment. Visceral disease responds over similar timeframes when imaging-monitored. Radiation-treated lesions regress over 4-12 weeks. Lymphedema improvement is slower and often partial, requiring sustained compression and rehabilitation. Pomalidomide responses develop over 2-4 cycles (8-16 weeks). Maintenance with continuous ART is required indefinitely; chemotherapy is held once best response is achieved and reintroduced on progression.
complications: Pulmonary KS — poor prognostic feature with median survival of 12-18 months without effective chemotherapy and ART
Gastrointestinal KS with chronic bleeding, iron-deficiency anemia, or obstruction
Severe lymphedema with skin breakdown, recurrent cellulitis, and reduced function
KS-associated multicentric Castleman disease (HHV-8-driven lymphoproliferation) and KSHV inflammatory cytokine syndrome (KICS) — severe systemic illnesses with high mortality
Cardiotoxicity from cumulative anthracycline exposure, particularly with conventional doxorubicin
Opportunistic infections in patients with persistent low CD4 counts despite ART

supportResources.name: National Cancer Institute — Kaposi Sarcoma Treatment (PDQ)
supportResources.description: Authoritative US National Cancer Institute patient and professional summary on KS treatment.

supportResources.name: AIDS Malignancy Consortium
supportResources.description: NCI-funded multicenter cooperative group for HIV-associated malignancy clinical trials and consensus guidelines.

supportResources.name: British HIV Association (BHIVA) Guidelines
supportResources.description: UK guidance on management of HIV-associated cancers including KS.

supportResources.name: CDC — Kaposi Sarcoma and HHV-8
supportResources.description: US public-health agency information for patients and clinicians.

confusedWithConditions.name: Melanoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Melanoma is a malignancy of melanocytes presenting as irregular pigmented lesions that change over months. KS lesions are vascular (red-purple, not pigmented), often multiple and symmetric, and biopsy shows spindle cells with vascular slits and HHV-8 LANA-1 positivity rather than malignant melanocytes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lymphoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lymphoma presents with lymphadenopathy, B symptoms, and biopsy showing lymphoid neoplasm. KS can mimic lymphoma when nodal involvement predominates (especially endemic pediatric form), but skin lesions, HHV-8 LANA-1 positivity, and vascular histology distinguish KS. Note that HHV-8 also causes primary effusion lymphoma in some HIV patients.

confusedWithConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: HIV/AIDS is the underlying immunosuppression that permits KS development. KS is one of the AIDS-defining illnesses. Many AIDS patients have KS; not all KS patients have HIV (classic, endemic, and iatrogenic forms exist). HIV testing is mandatory in any new KS diagnosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tuberculosis and KS frequently coexist in HIV-positive patients from endemic regions and can both cause pulmonary nodules and lymphadenopathy. Acid-fast bacilli, granulomatous histology, and chest imaging distinguish TB; vascular spindle-cell histology and HHV-8 LANA-1 distinguish KS. Treatment of both may be needed simultaneously.

relatedConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
relatedConditions.relevance: Underlying immunosuppression for AIDS-related KS; viral suppression on ART is the foundation of treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Common comorbidity in HIV-positive KS patients in endemic regions; requires concurrent management.

relatedConditions.name: Lymphoma
relatedConditions.relevance: HHV-8 also causes primary effusion lymphoma and KS-associated multicentric Castleman disease in some HIV-positive patients.

relatedConditions.name: Melanoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Another pigmented skin malignancy that enters the differential diagnosis; biopsy distinguishes them definitively.

faqs.question: What is Kaposi's sarcoma?
faqs.answer: Kaposi's sarcoma is a vascular cancer caused by human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8). It typically appears as red, purple, or brown skin lesions but can also involve the mouth, lymph nodes, gut, and lungs.

faqs.question: What causes Kaposi sarcoma?
faqs.answer: Kaposi sarcoma is caused by infection with human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8), also known as Kaposi sarcoma-associated herpesvirus (KSHV).

faqs.question: Is Kaposi sarcoma cancer?
faqs.answer: Yes. Kaposi sarcoma is classified as a low-grade vascular malignancy. Unlike many cancers it does not typically metastasize through bloodstream spread; instead, multiple new lesions develop because of widespread HHV-8 infection of endothelial cells.

faqs.question: Is Kaposi sarcoma always related to HIV?
faqs.answer: No. AIDS-related KS is the most common form globally, but classic KS (elderly Mediterranean men), endemic African KS (pre-HIV, all ages), and iatrogenic KS (organ transplant recipients) all occur without HIV infection. All forms share HHV-8 as the necessary infectious cause.

faqs.question: How is Kaposi sarcoma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires biopsy of a suspicious skin or mucosal lesion. Histology shows characteristic spindle cells, slit-like vascular channels, and red-cell extravasation.

faqs.question: What does Kaposi sarcoma look like?
faqs.answer: Kaposi sarcoma typically appears as red, purple, or brown patches, plaques, or nodules on the skin or mucous membranes. Lesions are often multiple and symmetric, can appear along skin tension lines, and are painless. Common sites include the lower legs, feet, face, hard palate, and gums.

faqs.question: How is Kaposi sarcoma treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on type and extent. For AIDS-related KS, antiretroviral therapy (ART) is the foundation — over 60% of limited-disease cases respond to ART alone. Extensive or visceral disease adds chemotherapy with liposomal doxorubicin or pomalidomide.

faqs.question: Can Kaposi sarcoma be cured?
faqs.answer: Limited Kaposi sarcoma can go into long-term remission, but HHV-8 remains in the body lifelong and recurrence is possible. AIDS-related KS responds dramatically to ART with sustained complete response in 60-80% of limited disease. Iatrogenic KS often fully regresses with sirolimus.

faqs.question: How is HHV-8 transmitted?
faqs.answer: HHV-8 transmits primarily through saliva and to a lesser extent through sexual contact and shared injection equipment. Mother-to-child transmission via saliva accounts for much endemic-area childhood infection. Casual contact does not transmit it.

faqs.question: What is the survival rate for Kaposi sarcoma?
faqs.answer: Modern survival is excellent in well-resourced settings: 5-year overall survival exceeds 75-85% for AIDS-related KS on ART. Classic KS has 10-year survival above 85%. Pulmonary KS and disseminated disease are higher-risk.

faqs.question: Can children get Kaposi sarcoma?
faqs.answer: Yes, particularly the endemic African form and AIDS-related KS in pediatric HIV. The lymphadenopathic pediatric form, common in equatorial Africa, presents with generalized lymphadenopathy without skin lesions and has a poor prognosis without prompt chemotherapy.

faqs.question: Why does Kaposi sarcoma cause swelling?
faqs.answer: Kaposi sarcoma lesions and KS-affected lymph nodes obstruct lymphatic drainage, causing lymphedema in the affected limb, face, or genitalia. Lymphedema can be severe, painful, and prone to recurrent cellulitis.

faqs.question: Does Kaposi sarcoma spread to organs?
faqs.answer: Yes. KS commonly involves lymph nodes, the gastrointestinal tract (often asymptomatic, found at endoscopy in up to 40% of extensive cutaneous cases), and the lungs (a poor prognostic site). Bone and central nervous system involvement are rare.

faqs.question: Can ART alone cure AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma?
faqs.answer: ART alone produces sustained complete or partial response in 60-80% of patients with limited skin-only (T0) AIDS-related KS. Extensive disease (T1) usually requires concurrent chemotherapy. ART should be started promptly — within 2 weeks of diagnosis — regardless of CD4 count.

faqs.question: What is pomalidomide for Kaposi sarcoma?
faqs.answer: Pomalidomide is an oral immunomodulator with anti-angiogenic and anti-inflammatory effects, approved by the FDA in 2020 for KS after standard therapy. It works in both HIV-positive and HIV-negative patients with response rates of 60-70%.

faqs.question: Can I prevent Kaposi sarcoma?
faqs.answer: The most effective prevention is preventing HIV infection (with PrEP, condoms, and harm reduction) and, for HIV-positive individuals, sustained ART with full viral suppression. Transplant recipients in HHV-8-endemic regions may benefit from sirolimus-based regimens when clinically feasible.

faqs.question: Does Kaposi sarcoma always appear on the skin?
faqs.answer: No. While most cases include skin involvement, pure visceral KS — particularly pulmonary or gastrointestinal — does occur, and lymphadenopathic pediatric African KS often has no skin lesions. Endoscopy, bronchoscopy, or imaging may be needed in patients with consistent symptoms but no skin findings.

faqs.question: Will Kaposi sarcoma lesions disappear with treatment?
faqs.answer: Many lesions flatten substantially and fade, but residual pigmentation (hemosiderin) often persists for months to years even after the active disease is gone. Radiation-treated lesions usually clear most completely.

faqs.question: Are men more likely to get Kaposi sarcoma than women?
faqs.answer: Yes. All forms show male predominance. Classic KS affects men 10-15 times more than women; AIDS-related KS affects men 5-10 times more, partly because HHV-8 transmission is high among men who have sex with men. Endemic African KS has the most balanced sex ratio but still favors men 2-3 fold.

faqs.question: Is HHV-8 the same as the herpes virus that causes cold sores?
faqs.answer: No. HHV-8 (Kaposi sarcoma-associated herpesvirus) is a gamma-2 herpesvirus, related to Epstein-Barr virus. Cold sores are caused by herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1), an alpha-herpesvirus. Both establish lifelong latent infections but cause different diseases through different mechanisms.

metaTitle: Kaposi's Sarcoma: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Kaposi's sarcoma: an HHV-8-driven vascular cancer most common in HIV. Symptoms, diagnosis, ART, liposomal doxorubicin, and pomalidomide reviewed.

images.altText: Multiple purple-brown Kaposi's sarcoma skin lesions on the lower leg of a patient with AIDS-related disease
images.caption: Multiple Kaposi's sarcoma cutaneous lesions on the lower leg — classic presentation of AIDS-related KS.

images.altText: Purple Kaposi's sarcoma lesion on the hard palate of a patient with HIV-associated Kaposi sarcoma
images.caption: Oral Kaposi's sarcoma — purple lesion on the hard palate, common in advanced AIDS-related KS.
